indicator_text: Responsible lobbying must consider the wider public interest, not only an organisation's needs narrowly defined; it should respect the interests and needs of people, communities and the environment, and lobbyists should be able to present a public interest case for their positions.
source_quote: Responsible lobbying must consider the wider public interest, not only an organisation's needs narrowly defined. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:111)

indicator_text: Responsible lobbying should support an evidence-based approach to policy making, clearly respecting independent, peer-reviewed science.
source_quote: Responsible lobbying should support an evidence-based approach to policy making, clearly respecting independent, peer-reviewed science. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:117)

indicator_text: Responsible lobbying must respect and not undermine existing public policy / human rights frameworks developed by international agencies, national and/or sub-national governments, and standard-setting bodies.
source_quote: Responsible lobbying must respect and not undermine existing public policy / human rights frameworks developed by international agencies, national and/or sub-national governments (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:120)

indicator_text: Political donations should at best be avoided; if made, they should be fully and transparently disclosed.
source_quote: Political donations should at best be avoided. If made, they should be fully and transparently disclosed (see Principle 2). (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:123)

indicator_text: Conflicts of interest should be identified and disclosed with clear policies in place — including credible dispute resolution — to manage them.
source_quote: Conflicts of interest should be identified and disclosed (see Principle 2) with clear policies in place – including credible dispute resolution – to manage them. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:126)
notes: Top-level recommendation 1.5. Footnote 2 (lines 144-148) defines conflict of interest.

indicator_text: The lobbying organisation should have clear codes of conduct to prevent bribery and corruption in its relations with public officials, including the offering and receiving of gifts, hospitality or other financial and in-kind incentives; codes apply to all in-house and intermediary lobbyists.
source_quote: The lobbying organisation should have clear codes of conduct to prevent bribery and corruption in its relations with public officials (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:130)
notes: Top-level recommendation 1.6. Codes apply to in-house and intermediary lobbyists, paid or unpaid.

indicator_text: Policies and processes for managing the "revolving door" should be in place, covering the hiring of former politicians and public officials, secondments or placement of staff into public sectors, and cooling-off periods where needed.
source_quote: Policies and processes for managing the 'revolving door' should be in place, covering the hiring of former politicians and public officials (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:136)

indicator_text: Disclose the nature of all direct and intermediary lobbying (paid or unpaid), including: identity of lobbyists engaged; identity of public sector officials engaged; issues addressed; outcomes sought; positions on sub-national, national and international policy frameworks; frequency of engagement; and ultimate beneficiaries.
source_quote: The nature of all direct and intermediary lobbying, paid or unpaid, including: a. The identity of direct and intermediary lobbyists engaged (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:161)
notes: Top-level recommendation 2.1 with seven sub-bullets (a-g) listing required disclosure fields. Kept as one indicator since the framework's atomization treats 2.1 as one item with sub-fields.

indicator_text: Disclose the monetary value of: spend on direct lobbying (including spend on intermediary lobbyists, paid or unpaid); spend on third-party organisation memberships (including the lobbying portion); and political donations (including in-kind), consistent with national frameworks.
source_quote: The monetary value of: a. Spend on direct lobbying, including spend on intermediary lobbyists, paid or unpaid (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:178)
notes: Top-level recommendation 2.2 with three sub-bullets (a-c) covering financial spend categories.

indicator_text: Disclose any materials shared with public sector recipients as part of lobbying activity, excluding commercially sensitive materials.
source_quote: Any materials that were shared with public sector recipients as part of lobbying activity, excluding commercially sensitive materials. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:189)

indicator_text: Disclose the full list of third-party organisation memberships, including board seats held.
source_quote: Full list of third-party organisation memberships, including board seats held. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:192)

indicator_text: Disclose policies, processes and approaches, and results of accountability mechanisms such as periodic audits, referenced under other Principles of this Framework.
source_quote: Policies, processes and approaches, and results of accountability mechanisms such as periodic audits, referenced under other Principles of this Framework. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:194)

indicator_text: All disclosures should be made at least annually following reporting cycles, ideally quarterly.
source_quote: All disclosures should be: a. made at least annually following reporting cycles (ideally quarterly) (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:198)
notes: Cross-cutting disclosure-quality requirement under Transparency (lettered a/b/c, applies to all transparency disclosures).

indicator_text: All disclosures should be publicly available in an easily accessible and identifiable location on the organisation's website, and where relevant promptly made available to public sector officials lobbied.
source_quote: publicly available in an easily accessible and identifiable location on the organisation's website (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:202)

indicator_text: All disclosures should be compiled in a dedicated report, report section or webpage, providing a total view of the organisation's material activities and issues, nationally and globally.
source_quote: compiled in a dedicated report, report section or webpage, providing a total view of the organisation's material activities and issues (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:206)

indicator_text: Responsible lobbyists will have controls over third-party membership organisations (such as trade associations): processes to identify positions across all geographies (taking into account credible media reports), and processes to review and proactively manage relationships, with clear escalation steps in cases of misalignment up to withdrawal.
source_quote: Responsible lobbyists will have controls over third-party membership organisations, such as trade associations (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:223)
notes: Top-level recommendation 3.1 with two sub-bullets (a-b) on identification and management processes.

indicator_text: Have controls over all lobbyists (in-house and intermediary, paid or unpaid) to ensure they understand and adhere to organisational policies — through codes of conduct, training, and regular performance assessment — with substantial public sanctions for those who contravene policies.
source_quote: controls over all lobbyists, in-house and intermediary, paid or unpaid, to ensure they understand and adhere to organisational policies (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:235)

indicator_text: There will be external stakeholder involvement in lobbying — for example stakeholder partners may be involved in setting lobbying positions, agreeing lobbying strategy, and reviewing performance.
source_quote: There will be external stakeholder involvement in lobbying; for example stakeholder partners may be involved in setting lobbying positions (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:253)

indicator_text: Support for and adherence to national laws, standards and any national codes of conduct for lobbyists, with any illegal and unethical activity duly reported to relevant legal or oversight authorities.
source_quote: Support for and adherence to national laws, standards and any national codes of conduct for lobbyists, and any illegal and unethical activity to be duly reported (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:258)

indicator_text: The organisation's Board to have clear oversight of lobbying policy positions, the lobbying processes and practices of the organisation itself, of intermediary lobbyists (paid or unpaid), and of the lobbying activity of third-party organisations it is a member of.
source_quote: The organisation's Board to have clear oversight of lobbying policy positions, the lobbying processes and practices of the organisation itself (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:262)

indicator_text: Periodic audits, the results of which should inform policies and be publicly reported, of: all public disclosures (for accuracy and completeness); all lobbying activities (for alignment); and controls over third-party membership organisations or intermediary lobbyists (for effectiveness).
source_quote: Periodic audits, the results of which should inform policies and be publicly reported (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:268)
notes: Top-level recommendation 4.4 with three sub-bullets (a-c) covering audit scope.

indicator_text: Internal whistleblowing mechanisms, offering all employees the chance to raise anonymously any concerns or breaches of the organisation's lobbying policies and codes of conduct, or illegal/unethical lobbying activity.
source_quote: Internal whistleblowing mechanisms, offering all employees the chance to raise anonymously any concerns or breaches (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:279)

indicator_text: Respect and safeguard the opportunity for interest groups to lobby — for example, not spending disproportionate resources on lobbying to crowd out other interests, regardless of legal caps on political donations.
source_quote: Respecting and safeguarding the opportunity for interest groups to lobby, for example not spending disproportionate resources on lobbying to crowd out other interests (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:296)

indicator_text: Pursue opportunities for multi-stakeholder partnerships, including companies and CSOs, to lobby together where a common position that supports key public interest outcomes can be found.
source_quote: Pursuing opportunities for multi-stakeholder partnerships, including companies and CSOs, to lobby together where a common position that supports key public interest outcomes can be found. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:300)

indicator_text: Cooperation between companies and CSOs to promote and support higher transparency and accountability standards from governments.
source_quote: Cooperation between companies and CSOs to promote and support higher transparency and accountability standards from governments. (papers/text/Carnstone_2020__responsible_lobbying_framework.txt:304)
